questions.question: Under semantic versioning, when should you bump the MAJOR version number?
options: When you fix a bug that doesn't change the API surface
When you add a new optional endpoint that doesn't break existing clients
When you make a change that is not backward compatible
When you add a new optional response field
questions.explanation: SemVer reserves MAJOR version bumps for incompatible (breaking) API changes. Bug fixes bump PATCH, backward-compatible additions bump MINOR.

questions.question: Which of the following changes is backward-compatible?
options: Removing a field from a JSON response
Adding a new optional field to a JSON response
Changing a field's type from string to integer
Making an optional request field required
questions.explanation: Adding a new optional field to a response is safe because existing consumers that don't know about it will simply ignore it. All the other options break existing consumers.

questions.question: Your API uses header-based versioning (Stripe-Version: 2024-01-15). You need to remove the 'phone' field from the user response. What is the correct sequence of actions?
options: Remove the field, then update the docs, then email consumers
Announce deprecation → add Deprecation/Sunset headers → keep old version working until sunset date → remove in a new dated version
Delete the field from the database and deploy immediately
Add a feature flag for the removal, then switch it on for all consumers at once
questions.explanation: The deprecation workflow is: announce → warn (headers) → sunset date → remove. The old version keeps working until the sunset date, then a new dated version omits the field. This gives consumers time to migrate.

questions.question: You need to rename a database column from 'name' to 'full_name' that backs a production API. Using the expand/contract pattern, what is the correct order?
options: Drop 'name', add 'full_name', backfill data, update API
Add 'full_name', update API to read from 'full_name' while writing both, backfill data, then drop 'name' after zero v1 traffic
Rename 'name' to 'full_name' in one ALTER TABLE and deploy the API change simultaneously
Create a new table, migrate all data, swap table names in a single deployment
questions.explanation: Expand/contract: (1) Expand — add new column alongside old, (2) Migrate — dual-write to both columns and backfill data while consumers move to the new column, (3) Contract — remove old column only when zero traffic uses it. At no point is the system in a broken state.

questions.question: Contract testing with Pact catches breaking changes at what point in the development cycle?
options: After the breaking change reaches production and consumers report errors
During integration testing when both consumer and provider run end-to-end tests together
In the provider's CI pipeline, before the breaking change is deployed, by verifying the consumer's contract against the provider's latest code
During code review, when a human reviewer checks the contract manually
questions.explanation: Consumer-driven contract tests run in the provider's CI. Each consumer publishes a contract describing what it expects. The provider verifies it can satisfy all consumer contracts on every build. A breaking change causes the provider's contract verification to fail before deployment.

questions.question: What is the primary disadvantage of URL path versioning (/v1/, /v2/) compared to header-based versioning?
options: It is harder for clients to implement
It violates the REST principle that a URL should represent a resource identity (the same resource has different URLs across versions)
It doesn't allow caching in proxies and CDNs
It requires changes to the server's load balancer configuration
questions.explanation: In REST, a URL identifies a resource. With path versioning, the same logical resource (e.g., user #42) has different URLs in v1 and v2, which breaks that principle. Header-based versioning keeps the URL the same and expresses the version via content negotiation.
